title: 'It gets very hard': Early heat wave hits Valley homeless population before cooling centers open - 12News
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Manipur
full_text:
An unusually early and intense heat wave is hitting the Valley, but key resources for unhoused people, like the Heat Relief Network map, are not yet available.
PHOENIX — An early-season heat wave is arriving earlier and stronger than usual in the Valley, and some key resources meant to help vulnerable residents are not yet available.
One of the biggest gaps is the region’s Heat Relief Network, a Valley-wide map that typically shows cooling centers and water distribution sites. The map is not expected to be live until May 1, limiting options for people trying to escape dangerous temperatures.
Francisco Garza, who has experienced homelessness for about a decade, said the early heat is already taking a toll.
“It gets very hard. When it gets real hot like this, it gets real hard,” Garza said.
He is especially concerned about his dog.
“He's got fur, so he gets hot eat a lot easier than I would,” he said.
While triple-digit temperatures are typical in summer, Garza said reaching them this early is unusual.
“It's getting real hot early and real fast,” he said. “I don't want him getting overheated."
Phoenix reached 105 degrees Thursday—nearly 25 degrees above average for this time of year.
Some cities are responding faster than others. In Mesa, several cooling centers have already opened:
Terros Health (Stapley Health Center - Primary Care)
Monday through Thursday, 7:30 a.m. to 8 p.m.; Friday and Saturday, 7:30 a.m. to 5 p.m.
Friends at First
15 E. 1st Ave. (Outreach Center); Monday and Thursday, noon to 3 p.m.
A New Leaf (MesaCAN)
635 E. Broadway; Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.
In Phoenix, officials said public libraries remain open year-round and can provide air-conditioned relief during the hottest parts of the day.
David Hondula, director of the city’s Office of Heat Response and Mitigation, said those spaces are critical.
“Having our facilities, our libraries, for example, open during the day is going to help us address the most critical portion of the day we're seeing the most dangerous conditions,” Hondula said.
In Tempe, nonprofit Arizona HUGS is working to fill the gap.
“I think that in situations like this, when weather comes out of nowhere, we have to, you know, work together to figure out a plan on how to adjust,” said founder Austin Davis.
Volunteers set up a cooling space at a local church on Thursday, providing water, ice, and shade.
“Everywhere you go, you see homeless people, and if you are feeling hot as someone who is housed, you know, imagine how the homeless are feeling,” said Sara Malkin. “...So this is very important to have somewhere that welcomes everyone, no matter where they come from.”
For Garza, access to those resources can make a difference.
“It's good to know that if I need to, I come over here and get him cool, instead of being out there and having him overheating,” he said.
He hopes the extreme heat does not arrive as early next year.
“Hopefully we're off the streets by then, because it's a bit hard,” Garza said.
AZ HUGS plans to continue its outreach throughout the weekend:
Friday: Tempe Public Library, 3500 S. Rural Road, from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m., with volunteers distributing ice and water outside.
Saturday: University Presbyterian Church, 139 E. Alameda Drive in Tempe, from 11 a.m. to 5 p.m.
